citations.proposition: Administrative area inspections are Fourth Amendment searches; reasonableness has no ready test 'other than by balancing the need to search against the invasion which the search entails' — the reasonableness-balancing root.

A seizure of property is 'some meaningful interference with an individual's possessory interests in that property'; the Amendment reaches only governmental action; under the private-search doctrine a government inspection within the scope of a prior private search invades no remaining privacy, and re-examination is permissible where there is 'a virtual certainty that nothing else of significance' would be revealed.
citations.pinpoint: 466 U.S. at 113 (seizure def.); 113-14 (governmental action); 115-17 (private search); 119 (virtual certainty)

citations.proposition: Government may not exceed the scope of a prior private search without independent justification; FBI's projection of films the private party had not actually viewed was a significant expansion and a separate, unlawful search. The private search 'merely frustrated that expectation in part. It did not . . . strip the remaining unfrustrated portion . . . of all Fourth Amendment protection.'

citations.case: Skinner v. Railway Labor Executives' Ass'n
citations.bluebook: Skinner v. Railway Labor Executives' Ass'n, 489 U.S. 602 (1989)
citations.proposition: The Fourth Amendment does not apply to a search/seizure 'effected by a private party on his own initiative,' but applies 'if the private party acted as an instrument or agent of the Government'; whether a private party is such an agent 'necessarily turns on the degree of the Government's participation in the private party's activities,' resolved 'in light of all the circumstances.' Anchors the state-action requirement.

A genuinely private search/seizure does not implicate the Fourth Amendment; whether conduct is private or attributable to the government is determined in light of all the circumstances (cited in Skinner and Walter for the state-action/private-search baseline).

Moving stereo equipment a few inches to read serial numbers was a new search requiring probable cause; even a minimal further intrusion is a search. Pull-quote (verbatim): 'there is nothing new in the realization that the Constitution sometimes insulates the criminality of a few in order to protect the privacy of us all.'
Reasonable-expectation-of-privacy theory of a search (cross-reference only; deep treatment owned by [[2 searches under 4th Amendment]]).
Trespass/physical-intrusion theory of a search revived (GPS tracker on vehicle) (cross-reference only; deep treatment owned by [[2 searches under 4th Amendment]]).

citations.bluebook: District of Columbia v. Heller, 554 U.S. 570 (2008)
citations.proposition: SECOND AMENDMENT case — NOT Fourth Amendment authority. Cited solely for the framing that the Bill of Rights' 'right of the people' was 'widely understood to codify a pre-existing right, rather than to fashion a new one' (verbatim confirmed at 554 U.S. at 592).

non_case_items.note: Constitutional text quoted verbatim; two clauses (reasonableness + warrant).
Instructor mnemonic — not case law, not citation-verified; owns its own practical page [[CREW]]. Used here as the reasonableness step only.

corrections.raw: Private search doctrine (cat out of the bag search), note: May be defeated by US V Jones
corrections.correct: CAPTURE ERROR. United States v. Jones, 565 U.S. 400 (2012) is the GPS/trespass-theory case; it has nothing to do with the private-search doctrine and does not 'defeat' it. The private-search doctrine is governed by United States v. Jacobsen, 466 U.S. 109 (1984) and scope-limited by Walter v. United States, 447 U.S. 649 (1980). The Jones reference was dropped from the private-search discussion and Jones is used only as the trespass-theory cross-reference.

corrections.raw: Govenment agent (on behalf of, direction, or significant encouragement or participation) US v Jacobsen
corrections.correct: The state-action / government-agent principle is correctly anchored to Skinner v. Railway Labor Executives' Ass'n, 489 U.S. 602, 614-15 (1989) ('instrument or agent of the Government'; 'turns on the degree of the Government's participation') and Coolidge v. New Hampshire, 403 U.S. 443, 487 (1971). Jacobsen states the governmental-action limit generally but the operative agency standard is articulated in Skinner/Coolidge. The two-part agency test (govt knowledge+acquiescence; private intent to assist LE) is circuit law and is labeled persuasive, not a SCOTUS holding.

corrections.raw: Can not exceed scope of private search (Walterson)
corrections.correct: Case name is Walter v. United States, 447 U.S. 649 (1980), not 'Walterson.'

corrections.raw: search -physical intrusion const protected area or intrusion upon a reasonable expectation of privacy.
corrections.correct: Stated correctly as the two-theory definition; the trespass theory requires a physical intrusion on a constitutionally protected area to obtain information (Jones), and the privacy theory requires invading a reasonable expectation of privacy (Katz). Deep treatment cross-linked to [[2 searches under 4th Amendment]] rather than duplicated.

corrections.raw: DC v Heller 2008 (codified pre-existing right) — listed among 4A foundational cases
corrections.correct: Heller is a SECOND AMENDMENT case, not Fourth Amendment authority. Kept out of the Key cases table and confined to a single labeled nuance bullet, cited only for the 'codify a pre-existing right' framing (verbatim confirmed).
